chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Translator's Note

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: They told me that I'm a traitor to my own profession,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: that I should be fired,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: had my medical license taken away,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: that I should go back to my own country.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: My email got hacked

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: in a discussion forum for other doctors.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Someone took credit for Twitter bombing my account.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Now, I didn't know if this was a good or bad thing.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: But then came the response.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Too bad it wasn't a real bomb.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: I never thought that I would do something

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: that would provoke this level of anger among other doctors.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Becoming a doctor was my dream.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: I grew up in China,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: and my earliest memories are of being rushed to the hospital

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: because I had such bad asthma that I was there nearly every week.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: I had this one doctor, Dr. Sam, who always took care of me.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: She was about the same age as my mother.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: She had this wild, curly hair,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: and she always wore these bright, yellow, flowery dresses.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: She was one of those doctors who, if you fell and you broke your arm,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: she'd ask you why you weren't laughing,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: because it's your humorous, get it?

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: See, you've grown,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: but she'd always make you feel better after having seen her.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Well, we all have that childhood hero

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: that we want to grow up to be just like, right?

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Well, I wanted to be just like Dr. Sam.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: When I was eight, my parents and I moved to the US,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: and ours became the typical immigrant narrative.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: My parents cleaned hotel rooms and washed dishes and pumped gas

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: so that I could pursue my dream.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Well, eventually, I learned enough English,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: and my parents were so happy the day that I got into medical school

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: and took my oath of healing and service.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: But then one day, everything changed.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: My mother called me to tell me that she wasn't feeling well,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: that she had this cough that wouldn't go away,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: that she was short of breath and tired.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Well, I knew that my mother was someone who never complained about anything.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: For her to tell me that something was the matter,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: I knew something had to be really wrong.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: And it was.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: We found out that she had stage four breast cancer,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: cancer that by then had spread to her lungs, her bones, and her brain.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: My mother was brave, though, and she had hope.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: She went through surgery and radiation

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: and was on her third round of chemotherapy,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: where she lost her address book.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: She tried to look up her oncologist's phone number on the internet,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: and she found it, but she found something else, too.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: On several websites,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: he was listed as a highly paid speaker to a drug company,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: and in fact, often spoke on behalf of the same chemo regimen

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: that he had prescribed her.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: She called me in a panic, and I didn't know what to believe.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Maybe this was the right chemo regimen for her,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: but maybe it wasn't.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: It made her scared, and it made her doubt.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: When it comes to medicine, having that trust is a must,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: and when that trust is gone, then all that's left is fear.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: There's another side to this fear.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: As a medical student, I was taking care of this 19-year-old

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: who was biking back to his dorm

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: when he got struck and hit, run over by an SUV.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: He had seven broken ribs, shattered hip bones,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: and he was bleeding inside his belly and inside his brain.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Now, imagine being his parents,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: who flew in from Seattle 2,000 miles away to find their son in a coma.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: You'd want to find out what's going on with him, right?

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: They asked to attend our bedside rounds

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: where we discussed his condition and his plan,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: which I thought was a pretty reasonable request

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: because it would give us a chance to show them

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: how much we were trying and how much we cared.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: The head doctor, though, said no.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: He gave all kinds of reasons.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Maybe they'll get in the nurse's way.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Maybe they'll stop students from asking questions.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: He even said,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: what if they see mistakes and sue us?

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: What I saw behind every excuse was deep fear,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: and what I learned was that to become a doctor,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: we have to put on our white coats,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: put up a wall, and hide behind it.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: There's a hidden epidemic in medicine.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Of course, patients are scared when they come to the doctor, right?

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: I mean, imagine you wake up with this terrible belly ache.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: You go to the hospital, you're lying in this strange place,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: you're in this hospital gurney, you're wearing this flimsy gown,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: strangers are coming to poke and prod at you.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: You don't know what's going to happen.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: You don't even know if you're going to get that blanket

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: that you asked for 30 minutes ago.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: But it's not just patients who are scared.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Doctors are scared, too.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: We're scared of patients finding out who we are

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: and what medicine is all about.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: And so what do we do?

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: We put on our white coats, and we hide behind them.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Of course, the more we hide,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: the more people want to know what it is that we're hiding,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: the more fear then spirals into mistrust and poor medical care.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: We don't just have a fear of sickness,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: we have a sickness of fear.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Can we bridge this disconnect

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: between what patients need and what doctors do?

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Can we overcome the sickness of fear?

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Well, let me ask you differently if hiding isn't the answer.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: What if we did the opposite?

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: What if doctors were to become totally transparent with our patients?

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Last fall, I conducted a research study

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: to find out what it is that people want to know about their healthcare.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: I didn't just want to study patients in a hospital, but everyday people.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: So my two medical students, Suhabhi Tucker and Laura Johns,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: literally took their research to the streets.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: They went to banks, coffee shops, senior centers,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Chinese restaurants, and train stations.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: What did they find?

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Well, when we asked people,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: what do you want to know about your healthcare,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: people responded with what they want to know about their doctors,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: because people understand healthcare

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: to be the individual interaction between them and their doctors.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: When we asked people, what do you want to know about your doctors,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: people gave three different answers.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Some want to know that their doctor is competent

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: and certified to practice medicine.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Some want to be sure that their doctor is unbiased

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: and is making decisions based on evidence and science,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: not on who pays them.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Surprisingly to us,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: many people want to know something else about their doctors.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Jonathan, a 28-year-old law student,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: says he wants to find someone who is comfortable with LGBTQ patients

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: and specializes in LGBT health.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Serena, a 32-year-old accountant,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: says that it's important to her for her doctor to share her values

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: when it comes to reproductive choice and women's rights.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Frank, a 59-year-old hardware store owner,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: doesn't even like going to the doctor

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: and wants to find someone who believes in prevention first

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: and who is comfortable with alternative treatments.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: One after another,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: our respondents told us that that doctor-patient relationship

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: is a deeply intimate one,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: that to show their doctors their bodies

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: and tell them their deepest secrets,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: they want to first understand their doctors' values.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Just because doctors have to see every patient

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: doesn't mean that patients have to see every doctor.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: People want to know about their doctors first

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: so that they can make informed choice.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: As a result of this, I formed a campaign,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Who's My Doctor?

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: that calls for total transparency in medicine.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Participating doctors voluntarily disclose on a public website

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: not just information about where we went to medical school

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: and what specialty we're in,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: but also our conflicts of interests.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: We go beyond the government sunshine act

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: about drug company affiliations,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: and we talk about how we're paid.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Incentives matter.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: If you go to your doctor because of back pain,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: you might want to know that he's getting paid $5,000 to perform spine surgery

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: versus $25 to refer you to see a physical therapist,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: or if he's getting paid the same thing,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: no matter what he recommends.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Then we go one step further.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: We add our values when it comes to women's health,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: LGBT health, alternative medicine, preventive health,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: and end-of-life decisions.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: We pledge to our patients that we are here to serve you,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: so you have a right to know who we are.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: We believe that transparency can be the cure for fear.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Well, I thought some doctors would sign on and others wouldn't,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: but I had no idea of the huge backlash that would ensue.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Within one week of starting Who's My Doctor?

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Medscape's public forum and several online doctors' communities

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: had thousands of posts about this topic.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Here are a few.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: From a gastroenterologist in Portland,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: I devoted 12 years of my life to being a slave.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: I have loans and mortgages.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: I depend on lunches from drug companies to serve patients.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Well, times may be hard for everyone,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: but try telling your patient,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: making $35,000 a year to serve a family of four,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: that you need the free lunch.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: From an orthopedic surgeon in Charlotte,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: I find it an evasion of my privacy

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: to disclose where my income comes from.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: My patients don't disclose their incomes to me.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: But your patient's sources of income don't affect your health.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: From a psychiatrist in New York City,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: pretty soon we will have to disclose whether we prefer cats to dogs,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: what model of car we drive, and what toilet paper we use.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Well, how you feel about Toyotas or Contenel

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: won't affect your patient's health.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: But your views on women's right to choose

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: and preventive medicine and end-of-life decisions just might.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: And my favorite, from a Kansas City cardiologist,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: more government-mandated stuff,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Dr. Wen needs to move back to her own country.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Well, two pieces of good news.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: First of all, this is meant to be voluntary and not mandatory.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: And second of all, I'm American, and I'm already here.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Within a month, my employers were getting calls asking for me to be fired.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: I received mail at my undisclosed home address

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: with threats to contact the medical board to sanction me.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: My friends and family urged me to quit this campaign.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: After the bomb threat, I was done.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: I was not going to be able to get out of the hospital.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: I was going to be in the hospital for the rest of my life.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: I was going to be in the hospital for the rest of my life.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: I was done.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: But then I heard from patients.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Over social media, a tweet chat, which I learned what that was by then,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: generated 4.3 million impressions,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: and thousands of people wrote to encourage me to continue.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: They wrote with things like,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: if doctors are doing something they're that ashamed of,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: they shouldn't be doing it.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Elected officials have to disclose campaign contributions.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Lawyers have to disclose conflicts of interests.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Why shouldn't doctors?

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: And finally, many people wrote and said,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: let us patients decide what's important when we're choosing a doctor.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: In our initial trial,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: over 300 doctors have taken the total transparency pledge.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: What a crazy new idea, right?

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: But actually, this is not that new of a concept at all.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Remember Dr. Sam, my doctor in China with the goofy jokes and the wild hair?

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Well, she was my doctor,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: but she was also our neighbor who lived in the building across the street.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: I went to the same school as her daughter.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: My parents and I trusted her because we knew who she was and what she stood for,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: and she had no need to hide from us.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Just one generation ago, this was the norm in the US as well.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: You knew that your family doctor was the father of two teenage boys,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: that he quit smoking a few years ago,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: that he says he's a regular churchgoer,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: but you see him twice a year,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: once on Easter, and once when his mother-in-law comes to town.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: You knew what he was about, and he had no need to hide from you.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: But the sickness of fear has taken over,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: and patients suffer the consequences.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: I know this firsthand.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: My mother fought her cancer for eight years.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: She was a planner,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: and she thought a lot about how she wanted to live and how she wanted to die.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Not only did she sign advanced directives,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: she wrote a 12-page document about how she had suffered enough,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: how it was time for her to go.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: One day, when I was a resident physician,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: I got a call to say that she was in the intensive care unit.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: By the time I got there,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: she was about to be intubated and put on a breathing machine.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: But this is not what she wants, I said, and we have documents.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: The ICU doctor looked me in the eye,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: pointed at my then 16-year-old sister and said,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: do you remember when you were that age?

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: How would you have liked to grow up without your mother?

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Her oncologist was there, too, and said,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: this is your mother.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Can you really face yourself for the rest of your life

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: if you don't do everything for her?

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: I knew my mother so well.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: I understood what her directives meant so well.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: And I was a physician.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: That was the single hardest decision I ever made,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: to let her die in peace.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: And I carry those words of those doctors with me every single day.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: We can bridge the disconnect between what doctors do

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: and what patients need.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: We can get there because we've been there before

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: and we know that transparency gets us to that trust.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Research has shown us that openness also helps doctors,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: that having open medical records,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: being willing to talk about medical errors

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: while increased patient trust,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: improve health outcomes,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: and reduce malpractice.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: That openness, that trust, is only going to be more important

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: as we move from the infectious to the behavioral model of disease.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Bacteria may not care so much about trust and intimacy,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: but for people to tackle the hard lifestyle choices

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: to address issues like smoking cessation,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: blood pressure management, and diabetes control,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: what that requires us to establish trust.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Here's what other transparent doctors have said.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Brandon Combs, an internist in Denver.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: This has brought me closer to my patients.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: The type of relationship I've developed,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: that's why I entered medicine.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Aaron Stuppel, an internist in Denver.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: I tell my patients that I am totally open with them.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: I don't hide anything from them.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: This is me.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Now tell me about you.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: We're in this together.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: May Nguyen, a family physician,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: My colleagues are astounded by what I'm doing.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: They ask me how I could be so brave.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: I said, I'm not being brave.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: It's my job.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: I leave you today with a final thought.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Being totally transparent is scary.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: You feel naked, exposed, and vulnerable.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: But that vulnerability, that humility,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: that trust, that trust,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: that humility,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: it can be an extraordinary benefit to the practice of medicine.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: When doctors are willing to step off our pedestals,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: take off our white coats,

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: and show our patients who we are and what medicine is all about.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: That's when we begin to overcome the sickness of fear.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: That's when we establish trust.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: That's when we change the paradigm of medicine

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: from one of secrecy and hiding

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: to one that is fully open and engaged for our patients.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Thank you.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Thank you.

chunks.title: What_your_doctor_won’t_disclose
chunks.text: Thank you.

text: Translator's Note They told me that I'm a traitor to my own profession, that I should be fired, had my medical license taken away, that I should go back to my own country. My email got hacked in a discussion forum for other doctors. Someone took credit for Twitter bombing my account. Now, I didn't know if this was a good or bad thing. But then came the response. Too bad it wasn't a real bomb. I never thought that I would do something that would provoke this level of anger among other doctors. Becoming a doctor was my dream. I grew up in China, and my earliest memories are of being rushed to the hospital because I had such bad asthma that I was there nearly every week. I had this one doctor, Dr. Sam, who always took care of me. She was about the same age as my mother. She had this wild, curly hair, and she always wore these bright, yellow, flowery dresses. She was one of those doctors who, if you fell and you broke your arm, she'd ask you why you weren't laughing, because it's your humorous, get it? See, you've grown, but she'd always make you feel better after having seen her. Well, we all have that childhood hero that we want to grow up to be just like, right? Well, I wanted to be just like Dr. Sam. When I was eight, my parents and I moved to the US, and ours became the typical immigrant narrative. My parents cleaned hotel rooms and washed dishes and pumped gas so that I could pursue my dream. Well, eventually, I learned enough English, and my parents were so happy the day that I got into medical school and took my oath of healing and service. But then one day, everything changed. My mother called me to tell me that she wasn't feeling well, that she had this cough that wouldn't go away, that she was short of breath and tired. Well, I knew that my mother was someone who never complained about anything. For her to tell me that something was the matter, I knew something had to be really wrong. And it was. We found out that she had stage four breast cancer, cancer that by then had spread to her lungs, her bones, and her brain. My mother was brave, though, and she had hope. She went through surgery and radiation and was on her third round of chemotherapy, where she lost her address book. She tried to look up her oncologist's phone number on the internet, and she found it, but she found something else, too. On several websites, he was listed as a highly paid speaker to a drug company, and in fact, often spoke on behalf of the same chemo regimen that he had prescribed her. She called me in a panic, and I didn't know what to believe. Maybe this was the right chemo regimen for her, but maybe it wasn't. It made her scared, and it made her doubt. When it comes to medicine, having that trust is a must, and when that trust is gone, then all that's left is fear. There's another side to this fear. As a medical student, I was taking care of this 19-year-old who was biking back to his dorm when he got struck and hit, run over by an SUV. He had seven broken ribs, shattered hip bones, and he was bleeding inside his belly and inside his brain. Now, imagine being his parents, who flew in from Seattle 2,000 miles away to find their son in a coma. You'd want to find out what's going on with him, right? They asked to attend our bedside rounds where we discussed his condition and his plan, which I thought was a pretty reasonable request because it would give us a chance to show them how much we were trying and how much we cared. The head doctor, though, said no. He gave all kinds of reasons. Maybe they'll get in the nurse's way. Maybe they'll stop students from asking questions. He even said, what if they see mistakes and sue us? What I saw behind every excuse was deep fear, and what I learned was that to become a doctor, we have to put on our white coats, put up a wall, and hide behind it. There's a hidden epidemic in medicine. Of course, patients are scared when they come to the doctor, right? I mean, imagine you wake up with this terrible belly ache. You go to the hospital, you're lying in this strange place, you're in this hospital gurney, you're wearing this flimsy gown, strangers are coming to poke and prod at you. You don't know what's going to happen. You don't even know if you're going to get that blanket that you asked for 30 minutes ago. But it's not just patients who are scared. Doctors are scared, too. We're scared of patients finding out who we are and what medicine is all about. And so what do we do? We put on our white coats, and we hide behind them. Of course, the more we hide, the more people want to know what it is that we're hiding, the more fear then spirals into mistrust and poor medical care. We don't just have a fear of sickness, we have a sickness of fear. Can we bridge this disconnect between what patients need and what doctors do? Can we overcome the sickness of fear? Well, let me ask you differently if hiding isn't the answer. What if we did the opposite? What if doctors were to become totally transparent with our patients? Last fall, I conducted a research study to find out what it is that people want to know about their healthcare. I didn't just want to study patients in a hospital, but everyday people. So my two medical students, Suhabhi Tucker and Laura Johns, literally took their research to the streets. They went to banks, coffee shops, senior centers, Chinese restaurants, and train stations. What did they find? Well, when we asked people, what do you want to know about your healthcare, people responded with what they want to know about their doctors, because people understand healthcare to be the individual interaction between them and their doctors. When we asked people, what do you want to know about your doctors, people gave three different answers. Some want to know that their doctor is competent and certified to practice medicine. Some want to be sure that their doctor is unbiased and is making decisions based on evidence and science, not on who pays them. Surprisingly to us, many people want to know something else about their doctors. Jonathan, a 28-year-old law student, says he wants to find someone who is comfortable with LGBTQ patients and specializes in LGBT health. Serena, a 32-year-old accountant, says that it's important to her for her doctor to share her values when it comes to reproductive choice and women's rights. Frank, a 59-year-old hardware store owner, doesn't even like going to the doctor and wants to find someone who believes in prevention first and who is comfortable with alternative treatments. One after another, our respondents told us that that doctor-patient relationship is a deeply intimate one, that to show their doctors their bodies and tell them their deepest secrets, they want to first understand their doctors' values. Just because doctors have to see every patient doesn't mean that patients have to see every doctor. People want to know about their doctors first so that they can make informed choice. As a result of this, I formed a campaign, Who's My Doctor? that calls for total transparency in medicine. Participating doctors voluntarily disclose on a public website not just information about where we went to medical school and what specialty we're in, but also our conflicts of interests. We go beyond the government sunshine act about drug company affiliations, and we talk about how we're paid. Incentives matter. If you go to your doctor because of back pain, you might want to know that he's getting paid $5,000 to perform spine surgery versus $25 to refer you to see a physical therapist, or if he's getting paid the same thing, no matter what he recommends. Then we go one step further. We add our values when it comes to women's health, LGBT health, alternative medicine, preventive health, and end-of-life decisions. We pledge to our patients that we are here to serve you, so you have a right to know who we are. We believe that transparency can be the cure for fear. Well, I thought some doctors would sign on and others wouldn't, but I had no idea of the huge backlash that would ensue. Within one week of starting Who's My Doctor? Medscape's public forum and several online doctors' communities had thousands of posts about this topic. Here are a few. From a gastroenterologist in Portland, I devoted 12 years of my life to being a slave. I have loans and mortgages. I depend on lunches from drug companies to serve patients. Well, times may be hard for everyone, but try telling your patient, making $35,000 a year to serve a family of four, that you need the free lunch. From an orthopedic surgeon in Charlotte, I find it an evasion of my privacy to disclose where my income comes from. My patients don't disclose their incomes to me. But your patient's sources of income don't affect your health. From a psychiatrist in New York City, pretty soon we will have to disclose whether we prefer cats to dogs, what model of car we drive, and what toilet paper we use. Well, how you feel about Toyotas or Contenel won't affect your patient's health. But your views on women's right to choose and preventive medicine and end-of-life decisions just might. And my favorite, from a Kansas City cardiologist, more government-mandated stuff, Dr. Wen needs to move back to her own country. Well, two pieces of good news. First of all, this is meant to be voluntary and not mandatory. And second of all, I'm American, and I'm already here. Within a month, my employers were getting calls asking for me to be fired. I received mail at my undisclosed home address with threats to contact the medical board to sanction me. My friends and family urged me to quit this campaign. After the bomb threat, I was done. I was not going to be able to get out of the hospital. I was going to be in the hospital for the rest of my life. I was going to be in the hospital for the rest of my life. I was done. But then I heard from patients. Over social media, a tweet chat, which I learned what that was by then, generated 4.3 million impressions, and thousands of people wrote to encourage me to continue. They wrote with things like, if doctors are doing something they're that ashamed of, they shouldn't be doing it. Elected officials have to disclose campaign contributions. Lawyers have to disclose conflicts of interests. Why shouldn't doctors? And finally, many people wrote and said, let us patients decide what's important when we're choosing a doctor. In our initial trial, over 300 doctors have taken the total transparency pledge. What a crazy new idea, right? But actually, this is not that new of a concept at all. Remember Dr. Sam, my doctor in China with the goofy jokes and the wild hair? Well, she was my doctor, but she was also our neighbor who lived in the building across the street. I went to the same school as her daughter. My parents and I trusted her because we knew who she was and what she stood for, and she had no need to hide from us. Just one generation ago, this was the norm in the US as well. You knew that your family doctor was the father of two teenage boys, that he quit smoking a few years ago, that he says he's a regular churchgoer, but you see him twice a year, once on Easter, and once when his mother-in-law comes to town. You knew what he was about, and he had no need to hide from you. But the sickness of fear has taken over, and patients suffer the consequences. I know this firsthand. My mother fought her cancer for eight years. She was a planner, and she thought a lot about how she wanted to live and how she wanted to die. Not only did she sign advanced directives, she wrote a 12-page document about how she had suffered enough, how it was time for her to go. One day, when I was a resident physician, I got a call to say that she was in the intensive care unit. By the time I got there, she was about to be intubated and put on a breathing machine. But this is not what she wants, I said, and we have documents. The ICU doctor looked me in the eye, pointed at my then 16-year-old sister and said, do you remember when you were that age? How would you have liked to grow up without your mother? Her oncologist was there, too, and said, this is your mother. Can you really face yourself for the rest of your life if you don't do everything for her? I knew my mother so well. I understood what her directives meant so well. And I was a physician. That was the single hardest decision I ever made, to let her die in peace. And I carry those words of those doctors with me every single day. We can bridge the disconnect between what doctors do and what patients need. We can get there because we've been there before and we know that transparency gets us to that trust. Research has shown us that openness also helps doctors, that having open medical records, being willing to talk about medical errors while increased patient trust, improve health outcomes, and reduce malpractice. That openness, that trust, is only going to be more important as we move from the infectious to the behavioral model of disease. Bacteria may not care so much about trust and intimacy, but for people to tackle the hard lifestyle choices to address issues like smoking cessation, blood pressure management, and diabetes control, what that requires us to establish trust. Here's what other transparent doctors have said. Brandon Combs, an internist in Denver. This has brought me closer to my patients. The type of relationship I've developed, that's why I entered medicine. Aaron Stuppel, an internist in Denver. I tell my patients that I am totally open with them. I don't hide anything from them. This is me. Now tell me about you. We're in this together. May Nguyen, a family physician, My colleagues are astounded by what I'm doing. They ask me how I could be so brave. I said, I'm not being brave. It's my job. I leave you today with a final thought. Being totally transparent is scary. You feel naked, exposed, and vulnerable. But that vulnerability, that humility, that trust, that trust, that humility, it can be an extraordinary benefit to the practice of medicine. When doctors are willing to step off our pedestals, take off our white coats, and show our patients who we are and what medicine is all about. That's when we begin to overcome the sickness of fear. That's when we establish trust. That's when we change the paradigm of medicine from one of secrecy and hiding to one that is fully open and engaged for our patients. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you.
